sections.biography-source-undefined:
Sun Hao was styled Yuanzong. He was the oldest son of Sun He and therefore a grandson to Sun Quan.

Sun He never became emperor because of controversial issues and he was forced to kill himself. Sun Hao was exiled along with his immediate family to Xindu.(SGZZ: Volume 59) Sun Hao was raised by his mother.

In 258, Sun Xiu enfeoffed Sun Hao as Marquis of Wucheng after the former became emperor. At this time, it is said that Sun Hao harbored imperial ambitions but did not dare reveal them. He made friends with the Prefect of Wucheng, Wan Yu and may have influenced the latter to lobby for him to take the throne after Sun Xiu died. Sun Xiu had four sons, all too young, and the officials wanted someone old enough to lead Wu against Wei, which had recently taken over Shu. Wan Yu told the powerful officials Puyang Xing and Zhang Bu that Sun Hao had virtue and might in the same way Sun Ce did, so he should succeed Sun Xiu. They agreed and convinced Empress Zhu, widow to Sun Xiu, to enthrone Sun Hao. Thus, Sun Hao succeeded Sun Xiu as Emperor in 264(SGZZ: Volume 48)

Sun Hao did not forget who had supported him and awarded them with titles. He also granted titles to his relatives: his wife, Lady Teng, was made Empress, his father-in-law, Teng Mu, was made Marquis of Gaomi, and enfeoffed three of his maternal uncles as marquises.(SGZZ: Volume 48) He posthumously elevated Sun He to emperor as Emperor Wen and moved his corpse to a mausoleum called Mingling.(SGZZ: Volume 59)

Sun Hao governed well, at first, lowering taxes and cutting spending expenditures by releasing concubines and animals in the imperial parks, and even gave financial relief to the needy. These hopes were dashed though as he soon revealed himself to be a cruel, lustful, arrogant, and greedy ruler. He was careless about moral standards. This unnerved Puyang Xing and Zhang Bu and they regretted enthroning Sun Hao. This got leaked and Sun Hao first had them exiled before sending assassins after them.(SGZZ: Volume 48)

Sima Zhao, who effectively ruled Wei, sent emissaries Xu Shao and Sun Yu in late 264 to persuade Sun Hao that there was no need to fight. Sun Hao accepted this and sent back an arrogant letter in early 265 which reads as follows: “I do know that you, as a talent for great generations, are in a position of a Chancellor, with the achievement of influencing and guiding, your diligence is also extreme! I am not virtuous but I have inherited the national governance, so I often think that me and the virtuous should together aid the way of the world, but because we are blocked from each other, I have not had been able to go along with that. Your admirable intentions are most obvious and I am going through your wishes deeply. Now I send Brilliantly Blessed Grandee; Ji Zhi as well as Household General for All Purposes; Hong Qiu to clearly announce the extent of my feelings."(SGZZ: Volume 48)

Xu Shao tried to return back to Wei and was going to Ruxu when Sun Hao summoned him back. Sun Hao then killed Xu Shao, who allegedly praised Wei, and exiled his family members to Jian’an commandery, which was a notorious place where the exiled were forced to build ships.(SGZZ: Volume 48) It was around this time Sun Hao began to take revenge on those who had wronged Sun He. He exiled the two sons of Sun Ba and their grandmother to Wuchang county, Kuaiji commandery for their relation to the deceased Sun Ba, who was the rival to Sun He.(SGZZ: Volume 59) Other officials who associated with Sun Ba were also exiled with their whole families.(SGZZ: Volume 48)

In the fourth lunar month of 265, a sweet dew was reported to be seen in the air in Jiangling, which the superstitious Sun Hao perceived as a good omen. He ordered that the era name be changed to Ganlu, which meant sweet dew, to reflect it.(SGZZ: Volume 48)

full_text:
Sun Hao (Yuanzong) 孫皓 (元宗)
ARomance of the Three KingdomsEncyclopedia officer profile.
Sun Hao (孫皓), styledYuanzong (元宗), was born in Fuchun county, Wu commandery. Last Emperor of Wu. Destroyed the country through tyranny and wasteful projects. Sun Hao was affiliated with the Wu Kingdom and the Jin Dynasty. Sun Hao lived during the Three Kingdoms and Jin Dynasty periods of Chinese history.
Return to theThree Kingdoms Encyclopediaor browse theEncyclopedia Directory.
